content:
# Exodus Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~37r–38r (PDF pp. 71–72); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 50–51 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_010_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_010_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_010_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Plague of Locusts — Arbeh (vv. 1–20)

**1** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Go in to Par'oh, for I have made heavy [kaved] his heart and the heart of his servants, so that I may set these my signs in his midst,

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה בֹּא אֶל־פַּרְעֹה כִּי־אֲנִי הִכְבַּדְתִּי אֶת־לִבּוֹ וְאֶת־לֵב עֲבָדָיו לְמַעַן שִׁתִי אֹתֹתַי אֵלֶּה בְּקִרְבּוֹ (bo el-Par'oh, "go in to Par'oh"; ki-ani hikhbadti et-libbo ve-et-lev avadav, "for I have made heavy his heart and the heart of his servants"; le-ma'an shiti ototai elleh be-qirbo, "so that I may set these my signs in his midst"). **CRITICAL — HARDENING VERB: kaved (כבד) = "make heavy." Hiphil הִכְבַּדְתִּי — YHWH is the AGENT.** This is the first time YHWH uses kaved (hiphil) to describe his own hardening action. Previously kaved hiphil was only used of Par'oh hardening himself (8:15, 8:32, 9:34). Now YHWH claims the kaved root as well, not only chazaq (9:12). The word אֹתֹתַי (ototai) = "my signs" — from אוֹת (ot, "sign"), the plagues as divine signs. LXX (Vaticanus): εἴσελθε πρὸς Φαραω· ἐγὼ γὰρ ἐσκλήρυνα αὐτοῦ τὴν καρδίαν καὶ τῶν θεραπόντων αὐτοῦ ἵνα ἑξῆς ἐπέλθῃ τὰ σημεῖα ταῦτα ἐπ᾽ αὐτούς ("go in to Pharao, for I have hardened his heart and the heart of his servants, that these signs may come upon them in succession") — σκληρύνω ("harden") with ἐγώ as subject; σημεῖα ("signs") for otot.

**2** and so that you may tell in the ears of your son and the son of your son what I have wrought [made sport] in Mitsrayim, and my signs which I have set among them; and you shall know that I [am] YHWH."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּלְמַעַן תְּסַפֵּר בְּאָזְנֵי בִנְךָ וּבֶן־בִּנְךָ אֵת אֲשֶׁר הִתְעַלַּלְתִּי בְּמִצְרַיִם וְאֶת־אֹתֹתַי אֲשֶׁר־שַׂמְתִּי בָם וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה (u-le-ma'an tesapper be-oznei vinkha u-ven-binkha, "and so that you may tell in the ears of your son and the son of your son"; et asher hit'alalti be-Mitsrayim, "what I have wrought/made sport in Mitsrayim"; vi-da'tem ki-ani YHWH, "and you shall know that I [am] YHWH"). **Intergenerational purpose:** The plagues are not only for Par'oh but for Yisra'el's memory. The verb הִתְעַלַּלְתִּי (hit'alalti) is hitpael of עלל — meaning "I have dealt severely with, made sport of, wrought wonders upon." The root עלל can mean "to act/deal" or "to make a fool of" — YHWH's actions in Mitsrayim are both judgment and mockery of Egyptian power. The phrase בְּאָזְנֵי בִנְךָ (be-oznei vinkha, "in the ears of your son") — literally "in the ears," emphasizing oral transmission. LXX: ὅπως διηγήσησθε εἰς τὰ ὦτα τῶν τέκνων ὑμῶν καὶ τοῖς τέκνοις τῶν τέκνων ὑμῶν ὅσα ἐμπέπαιχα τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις ("that you may tell in the ears of your children and your children's children what I have mocked among the Egyptians") — ἐμπαίζω ("mock, make sport of") for hit'alalti.

**3** And Mosheh and Aharon came in to Par'oh, and they said to him: "Thus says YHWH, the God of the Ivrim [Hebrews]: 'How long do you refuse to humble yourself before me? Send out my people, and let them serve me.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו כֹּה־אָמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים עַד־מָתַי מֵאַנְתָּ לֵעָנֹת מִפָּנָי שַׁלַּח עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי (ad-matai me'anta le'anot mippanai, "how long do you refuse to humble yourself before me"; shalakh ammi ve-ya'avduni, "send out my people and let them serve me"). The verb לֵעָנֹת (le'anot) is the qal infinitive of עָנָה II (anah, "to be bowed down, humble oneself") — Par'oh's refusal to humble himself contrasts with the demand that he "send out" (שַׁלַּח, shalakh). LXX: ἕως τίνος οὐ βούλει ἐντραπῆναι με; ("how long do you not wish to be ashamed/humbled before me?") — ἐντρέπομαι ("be ashamed, submit") for anah.

**4** For if you [are] refusing to send out my people, behold, I [am] bringing tomorrow arbeh [locusts] into your territory.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי אִם־מָאֵן אַתָּה לְשַׁלֵּחַ אֶת־עַמִּי הִנְנִי מֵבִיא מָחָר אַרְבֶּה בִּגְבֻלֶךָ (ki im-ma'en attah le-shale'akh et-ammi, "for if you [are] refusing to send out my people"; hineni mevi makhar arbeh bi-gevulekha, "behold, I [am] bringing tomorrow locusts into your territory"). The word אַרְבֶּה (arbeh) = "locust, swarming locust" — from רבה (ravah, "to be many, multiply"), so named for its swarming multitude. The word גְּבוּל (gevul) = "border, territory" — the entire domain of Par'oh. LXX: ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἐπάγω ταύτην τὴν ὥραν αὔριον ἀκρίδα πολλὴν ἐπὶ πάντα τὰ ὅριά σου ("behold, I bring at this hour tomorrow a great locust upon all your borders") — ἀκρίς ("locust") for arbeh.

**5** And it shall cover the eye of the land, and one shall not be able to see the land; and it shall eat the remnant of the escape — that which remains to you from the hail — and it shall eat every tree that sprouts for you from the field.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִסָּה אֶת־עֵין הָאָרֶץ וְלֹא יוּכַל לִרְאֹת אֶת־הָאָרֶץ וְאָכַל אֶת־יֶתֶר הַפְּלֵטָה הַנִּשְׁאֶרֶת לָכֶם מִן־הַבָּרָד וְאָכַל אֶת־כָּל־הָעֵץ הַצֹּמֵחַ לָכֶם מִן־הַשָּׂדֶה (ve-khissah et-ein ha-arets, "and it shall cover the eye of the land"; ve-lo yukhal lir'ot et-ha-arets, "and one shall not be able to see the land"; ve-akhal et-yeter ha-peletah ha-nish'eret lakhem min-ha-barad, "and it shall eat the remnant of the escape that remains to you from the hail"; ve-akhal et-kol-ha-ets ha-tsomea'kh lakhem min-ha-sadeh, "and it shall eat every tree that sprouts for you from the field"). **Key phrase:** עֵין הָאָרֶץ (ein ha-arets) = literally "the eye of the land" — its visible surface, its face. The phrase יֶתֶר הַפְּלֵטָה (yeter ha-peletah, "the remnant of the escape") refers to what survived the hail (ch. 9) — the wheat and spelt (9:32) that were late-ripening. LXX: καὶ καλύψει τὴν ὄψιν τῆς γῆς ("and it shall cover the face of the land") — ὄψις ("face, appearance") for ein; καὶ κατέδεται πᾶν τὸ περισσὸν τῆς γῆς τὸ καταλειφθέν ("and it shall devour all the surplus of the land that was left") — for yeter ha-peletah.

**6** And they shall fill your houses, and the houses of all your servants, and the houses of all Mitsrayim, which your fathers have not seen, nor your fathers' fathers, from the day they were upon the adamah [ground] until this day.'" And he turned and went out from Par'oh.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּמָלְאוּ בָתֶּיךָ וּבָתֵּי כָל־עֲבָדֶיךָ וּבָתֵּי כָל־מִצְרַיִם אֲשֶׁר לֹא־רָאוּ אֲבֹתֶיךָ וַאֲבוֹת אֲבֹתֶיךָ מִיּוֹם הֱיוֹתָם עַל־הָאֲדָמָה עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה וַיִּפֶן וַיֵּצֵא מֵעִם פַּרְעֹה (u-mal'u vatteicha u-vattei khol-avadeicha u-vattei khol-Mitsrayim, "and they shall fill your houses and the houses of all your servants and the houses of all Mitsrayim"; asher lo-ra'u avoteicha va-avot avoteicha, "which your fathers have not seen, nor your fathers' fathers"; mi-yom heyotam al-ha-adamah ad ha-yom ha-zeh, "from the day they were upon the ground until this day"; vayifen vayetse me-im Par'oh, "and he turned and went out from Par'oh"). The locust plague exceeds all precedent in Egyptian memory — a parallel to the hail (9:18, 24). The sudden departure (וַיִּפֶן, vayifen, "and he turned") without waiting for a response signals Mosheh's authority. LXX: καὶ πλησθήσονταί σου αἱ οἰκίαι καὶ αἱ οἰκίαι τῶν θεραπόντων σου καὶ πᾶσαι αἱ οἰκίαι ἐν πάσῃ γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ ("and your houses shall be filled, and the houses of your servants, and all the houses in all the land of Egypt").

**7** And the servants of Par'oh said to him: "How long shall this one be to us for a snare? Send out the men and let them serve YHWH their God. Do you not yet know that Mitsrayim is lost?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמְרוּ עַבְדֵי פַרְעֹה אֵלָיו עַד־מָתַי יִהְיֶה זֶה לָנוּ לְמוֹקֵשׁ שַׁלַּח אֶת־הָאֲנָשִׁים וְיַעַבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם הֲטֶרֶם תֵּדַע כִּי אָבְדָה מִצְרָיִם (ad-matai yihyeh zeh lanu le-moqesh, "how long shall this one be to us for a snare"; shalakh et-ha-anashim ve-ya'avdu et-YHWH Eloheihem, "send out the men and let them serve YHWH their God"; ha-terem teda' ki avdah Mitsrayim, "do you not yet know that Mitsrayim is lost/destroyed?"). **Narrative turning point:** Par'oh's own servants now echo the demand — the moqesh (מוֹקֵשׁ, "snare, trap") language treats Mosheh/the Israelite presence as a source of entrapment. The verb אָבְדָה (avdah) from אבד ("to perish, be lost") — Mitsrayim is being destroyed. The servants' question ha-terem teda' ("do you not yet know?") ironically echoes Mosheh's terem tire'un ("you do not yet fear") from 9:30. LXX: ἕως τίνος ἔσται τοῦτο ἡμῖν σκῶλον; ("how long shall this be for us a stumbling-block?") — σκῶλον for moqesh.

**8** And Mosheh and Aharon were brought back to Par'oh, and he said to them: "Go, serve YHWH your God. Who and who [are] the ones going?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיּוּשַׁב אֶת־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶת־אַהֲרֹן אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם לְכוּ עִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם מִי וָמִי הַהֹלְכִים (vayushav et-Mosheh ve-et-Aharon el-Par'oh, "and Mosheh and Aharon were brought back to Par'oh" — hophal of שׁוּב, "caused to return"; lekhu ivdu et-YHWH Eloheikhem, "go, serve YHWH your God"; mi va-mi ha-holkhim, "who and who [are] the ones going?"). The hophal verb וַיּוּשַׁב (vayushav) is passive — Par'oh summons them back after their dramatic exit (v.6). His question מִי וָמִי (mi va-mi, "who and who?") demands specification — he wants to negotiate the terms. LXX: καὶ ἀπέστρεψαν τόν τε Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων πρὸς Φαραω ("and they turned back both Moses and Aaron to Pharao"); τίνες καὶ τίνες εἰσὶν οἱ πορευόμενοι; ("who and who are the ones going?").

**9** And Mosheh said: "With our young and with our old we will go; with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds we will go, for [it is] a khag [feast/pilgrimage] of YHWH for us."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה בִּנְעָרֵינוּ וּבִזְקֵנֵינוּ נֵלֵךְ בְּבָנֵינוּ וּבִבְנוֹתֵנוּ בְּצֹאנֵנוּ וּבִבְקָרֵנוּ נֵלֵךְ כִּי חַג־יְהוָה לָנוּ (bi-ne'areinu u-vi-zqeneinu nelekh, "with our young and with our old we will go"; be-vaneinu u-vi-vnoteinu, "with our sons and with our daughters"; be-tso'neinu u-vi-vqareinu nelekh, "with our flocks and with our herds we will go"; ki khag-YHWH lanu, "for [it is] a feast of YHWH for us"). Mosheh's answer is comprehensive — all ages and all livestock, a total exodus. The word חַג (khag) = "feast, pilgrimage festival" — from חגג (khagag, "to make a pilgrimage, keep a festival"). LXX: σὺν τοῖς νεανίσκοις καὶ πρεσβυτέροις πορευσόμεθα, σὺν τοῖς υἱοῖς καὶ θυγατράσιν ("with the young men and elders we will go, with the sons and daughters").

**10** And he said to them: "May YHWH be so with you, as I will send you out and your little ones! See, for evil [is] before your faces.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יְהִי כֵן יְהוָה עִמָּכֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר אֲשַׁלַּח אֶתְכֶם וְאֶת־טַפְּכֶם רְאוּ כִּי רָעָה נֶגֶד פְּנֵיכֶם (yehi khen YHWH immakhem ka-asher ashale'akh etkhem ve-et-tappekhem, "may YHWH be so with you as I will send you out and your little ones" — a sarcastic oath meaning "YHWH will no more be with you than I will let your children go"; re'u ki ra'ah neged peneikhem, "see, for evil [is] before your faces"). **Par'oh's sarcasm and threat:** The phrase יְהִי כֵן יְהוָה עִמָּכֶם (yehi khen YHWH immakhem) is an ironic invocation — Par'oh uses YHWH's name in a mocking oath. The word רָעָה (ra'ah) = "evil, calamity" — Par'oh accuses them of plotting evil, or warns that evil will befall them. LXX: ἴδετε ὅτι πονηρία πρόσκειται ὑμῖν ("see that evil lies before you") — πονηρία for ra'ah.

**11** Not so! Go, please, the men [gevarim], and serve YHWH, for that [is] what you [are] seeking." And he drove them out from the face of Par'oh.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא כֵן לְכוּ־נָא הַגְּבָרִים וְעִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה כִּי אֹתָהּ אַתֶּם מְבַקְשִׁים וַיְגָרֶשׁ אֹתָם מֵאֵת פְּנֵי פַרְעֹה (lo khen lekhu-na ha-gevarim ve-ivdu et-YHWH, "not so! go, please, the men, and serve YHWH"; ki otah attem mevaqshim, "for that [is] what you [are] seeking"; vayegaresh otam me-et penei Far'oh, "and he drove them out from the face of Par'oh"). Par'oh's counter-offer: only adult men may go. The word גְּבָרִים (gevarim) = "men, strong men" — from גֶּבֶר (gever, "man, warrior"). The verb וַיְגָרֶשׁ (vayegaresh) = piel of גרשׁ, "he drove out" — a forceful expulsion from the royal presence. LXX: μὴ οὕτως· πορευέσθωσαν δὲ οἱ ἄνδρες ("not so! but let the men go"); καὶ ἐξέβαλεν αὐτοὺς ἀπὸ προσώπου Φαραω ("and he cast them out from the face of Pharao").

**12** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Stretch out your hand over the land of Mitsrayim for the arbeh [locust], and it shall go up upon the land of Mitsrayim and shall eat every herb of the land — all that the hail left."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה נְטֵה יָדְךָ עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בָּאַרְבֶּה וְיַעַל עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וְיֹאכַל אֶת־כָּל־עֵשֶׂב הָאָרֶץ אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר הִשְׁאִיר הַבָּרָד (neteh yadkha al-erets Mitsrayim ba-arbeh, "stretch out your hand over the land of Mitsrayim for the locust"; ve-ya'al al-erets Mitsrayim, "and it shall go up upon the land of Mitsrayim"; ve-yokhal et-kol-esev ha-arets, "and it shall eat every herb of the land"; et kol-asher hish'ir ha-barad, "all that the hail left"). The hail-locust sequence: what the hail spared, the locust devours. The verb הִשְׁאִיר (hish'ir) = hiphil of שׁאר, "left remaining." LXX: ἔκτεινον τὴν χεῖρά σου ἐπὶ γῆν Αἰγύπτου... καὶ κατέδεται πᾶσαν βοτάνην τῆς γῆς ("stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt... and it shall devour every plant of the land").

**13** And Mosheh stretched out his rod over the land of Mitsrayim, and YHWH drove an east wind [ru'akh qadim] in the land all that day and all the night; the morning came, and the east wind carried the arbeh [locust].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־מַטֵּהוּ עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וַיהוָה נִהַג רוּחַ קָדִים בָּאָרֶץ כָּל־הַיּוֹם הַהוּא וְכָל־הַלָּיְלָה הַבֹּקֶר הָיָה וְרוּחַ הַקָּדִים נָשָׂא אֶת־הָאַרְבֶּה (va-YHWH nihag ru'akh qadim ba-arets, "and YHWH drove an east wind in the land"; kol-ha-yom ha-hu ve-khol-ha-lailah, "all that day and all the night"; ha-boqer hayah ve-ru'akh ha-qadim nasa et-ha-arbeh, "the morning came and the east wind carried the locust"). The verb נִהַג (nihag) = "drove, guided" — from נהג ("to drive, lead"), the same verb used for driving flocks. The רוּחַ קָדִים (ru'akh qadim) = "east wind" — from the desert, the hot sirocco wind. YHWH uses natural means (wind) as instrument of plague. LXX: καὶ κύριος ἐπήγαγεν ἄνεμον νότον ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ("and the Lord brought a south wind upon the land") — νότος ("south wind") rather than "east wind," reflecting LXX geography (wind from the desert direction relative to Egypt). ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν καὶ ὅλην τὴν νύκτα ("the whole day and the whole night").

**14** And the arbeh [locust] went up upon all the land of Mitsrayim, and it rested in all the territory of Mitsrayim — very heavy [kaved]; before it there had not been arbeh like it, and after it there will not be so.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעַל הָאַרְבֶּה עַל כָּל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וַיָּנַח בְּכֹל גְּבוּל מִצְרָיִם כָּבֵד מְאֹד לְפָנָיו לֹא־הָיָה כֵן אַרְבֶּה כָּמֹהוּ וְאַחֲרָיו לֹא יִהְיֶה־כֵּן (vaya'al ha-arbeh al kol-erets Mitsrayim, "and the locust went up upon all the land of Mitsrayim"; vayanakh be-khol gevul Mitsrayim, "and it rested in all the territory of Mitsrayim"; kaved me'od, "very heavy"; lefanav lo-hayah khen arbeh kamohu, "before it there had not been so, locust like it"; ve-akharav lo yihyeh-ken, "and after it there will not be so"). The verb וַיָּנַח (vayanakh) from נוח ("to rest, settle") — the locusts "resting" upon the land is an ironic inversion of divine rest. The adjective כָּבֵד (kaved) again — the plague is "heavy" just as Par'oh's heart is "heavy." The uniqueness formula (before/after) parallels the hail (9:18, 24). **Note on Joel 2:2:** The prophet Yo'el will later echo this formula for an eschatological locust plague. LXX: καὶ ἀνέβη ἡ ἀκρὶς ἐπὶ πᾶσαν γῆν Αἰγύπτου... πρὸ αὐτῆς οὐ γέγονεν τοιαύτη ἀκρίς, καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα οὐκ ἔσται οὕτως ("and the locust went up upon all the land of Egypt... before it there had not been such a locust, and after this there will not be so").

**15** And it covered the eye of all the land, and the land was darkened, and it ate every herb of the land and every fruit of the tree which the hail had left; and there remained not any green thing in the tree or in the herb of the field in all the land of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְכַס אֶת־עֵין כָּל־הָאָרֶץ וַתֶּחְשַׁךְ הָאָרֶץ וַיֹּאכַל אֶת־כָּל־עֵשֶׂב הָאָרֶץ וְאֵת כָּל־פְּרִי הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר הוֹתִיר הַבָּרָד וְלֹא־נוֹתַר כָּל־יֶרֶק בָּעֵץ וּבְעֵשֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶה בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (vayekhas et-ein kol-ha-arets, "and it covered the eye of all the land"; va-tekhshakh ha-arets, "and the land was darkened" — from חשׁך, chashakh, "to be dark"; ve-lo notar kol-yereq ba-ets, "and there remained not any green thing in the tree"). **Foreshadowing darkness:** The verb וַתֶּחְשַׁךְ (va-tekhshakh, "and it was darkened") from חשׁך (choshekh, "darkness") — the locusts' covering of the land produces a darkness that anticipates the ninth plague. The word יֶרֶק (yereq) = "green, greenness" — total destruction of vegetation. LXX: καὶ ἐκάλυψεν τὴν ὄψιν τῆς γῆς, καὶ ἐφθάρη ἡ γῆ ("and it covered the face of the land, and the land was destroyed") — ἐφθάρη ("was destroyed/corrupted") rather than "was darkened."

**16** And Par'oh hastened to call to Mosheh and to Aharon, and he said: "I have sinned against YHWH your God and against you.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְמַהֵר פַּרְעֹה לִקְרֹא לְמֹשֶׁה וּלְאַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמֶר חָטָאתִי לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וְלָכֶם (vayemaher Par'oh liqro le-Mosheh u-le-Aharon, "and Par'oh hastened to call to Mosheh and to Aharon"; khatati la-YHWH Eloheikhem ve-lakhem, "I have sinned against YHWH your God and against you"). The verb וַיְמַהֵר (vayemaher) = piel of מהר, "he hastened" — urgency and desperation. Par'oh's confession (חָטָאתִי, khatati, "I have sinned") echoes 9:27, but now adds "against you" — sinning against YHWH's representatives. Yet he says "your God" (אֱלֹהֵיכֶם), not "my God." LXX: κατέσπευσεν Φαραω καλέσαι Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων ("Pharao hastened to call Moses and Aaron"); ἡμάρτηκα ἐναντίον κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ ὑμῶν καὶ εἰς ὑμᾶς ("I have sinned before the Lord your God and against you").

**17** And now, bear, please, my sin only this time, and pray to YHWH your God, and let him turn away from upon me only this death."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעַתָּה שָׂא נָא חַטָּאתִי אַךְ הַפַּעַם וְהַעְתִּירוּ לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וְיָסֵר מֵעָלַי רַק אֶת־הַמָּוֶת הַזֶּה (ve-attah sa na khattati akh ha-pa'am, "and now, bear, please, my sin only this time"; ve-ha'tiru la-YHWH Eloheikhem, "and pray to YHWH your God"; ve-yaser me-alai raq et-ha-mavet ha-zeh, "and let him turn away from upon me only this death"). Par'oh calls the locust plague הַמָּוֶת הַזֶּה (ha-mavet ha-zeh, "this death") — the locusts represent death to the land and its people through famine. The verb שָׂא (sa) = "bear, carry, lift" — a plea for forgiveness (to "carry away" sin). LXX: προσδέξασθε οὖν μου τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ("accept therefore my sin"); ἀφελέτω ἀπ᾽ ἐμοῦ τὸν θάνατον τοῦτον ("let him take away from me this death").

**18** And he went out from Par'oh, and he prayed to YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּצֵא מֵעִם פַּרְעֹה וַיֶּעְתַּר אֶל־יְהוָה (vayetse me-im Par'oh vaye'tar el-YHWH, "and he went out from Par'oh and he prayed to YHWH"). The verb וַיֶּעְתַּר (vaye'tar) from עתר ("to pray, entreat, supplicate") — intensive prayer. LXX: καὶ ἐξῆλθεν Μωυσῆς ἀπὸ Φαραω καὶ ηὔξατο πρὸς τὸν θεόν ("and Moses went out from Pharao and prayed to God").

**19** And YHWH turned a wind of the sea [yam], very strong, and it carried the arbeh [locust] and thrust it toward the Yam Suf [Sea of Reeds]; there remained not one arbeh in all the territory of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּהֲפֹךְ יְהוָה רוּחַ־יָם כָּבֵד מְאֹד וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת־הָאַרְבֶּה וַיִּתְקָעֵהוּ יָמָּה סּוּף לֹא נִשְׁאַר אַרְבֶּה אֶחָד בְּכֹל גְּבוּל מִצְרָיִם (vayahafokh YHWH ru'akh-yam kaved me'od, "and YHWH turned a wind of the sea, very strong"; vayissa et-ha-arbeh, "and it carried the locust"; vayitqa'ehu yammah Suf, "and it thrust it toward the Sea of Reeds"; lo nish'ar arbeh ekhad be-khol gevul Mitsrayim, "there remained not one locust in all the territory of Mitsrayim"). The רוּחַ־יָם (ru'akh-yam) = "west wind" (literally "wind of the sea," the Mediterranean). YHWH "turns" (הָפַךְ, hafakh) the wind — the same verb used for divine reversal and overthrow (as in Sodom). The verb וַיִּתְקָעֵהוּ (vayitqa'ehu) from תקע ("to thrust, drive, fasten") — forcefully driving the locusts into the יַם־סוּף (Yam Suf, "Sea of Reeds") — the same body of water that will feature in the exodus (ch. 14–15). LXX: καὶ μετέβαλεν κύριος ἄνεμον ἀπὸ θαλάσσης σφοδρόν ("and the Lord turned a wind from the sea, violent"); καὶ ἐνέβαλεν αὐτὴν εἰς τὴν ἐρυθρὰν θάλασσαν ("and cast it into the Red Sea") — ἐρυθρὰ θάλασσα ("Red Sea") for Yam Suf.

**20** And YHWH strengthened [chazaq] the heart of Par'oh, and he did not send out the sons of Yisra'el.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא שִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (vayekhazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh ve-lo shilakh et-benei Yisra'el, "and YHWH strengthened the heart of Par'oh, and he did not send out the sons of Yisra'el"). **HARDENING VERB: chazaq (חזק) = "strengthen." Piel וַיְחַזֵּק — YHWH is the AGENT.** This continues the pattern established in 9:12 — YHWH actively and intensively strengthens Par'oh's heart. No qualifying clause "as YHWH had spoken" appears here, making the statement more stark. LXX: καὶ ἐσκλήρυνεν κύριος τὴν καρδίαν Φαραω ("and the Lord hardened the heart of Pharao") — σκληρύνω with κύριος as subject.

---

## Plague of Darkness — Choshekh (vv. 21–29)

**21** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Stretch out your hand toward the heavens, and there shall be choshekh [darkness] upon the land of Mitsrayim — and one may feel [the] darkness [veyamesh choshekh]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה נְטֵה יָדְךָ עַל־הַשָּׁמַיִם וִיהִי חֹשֶׁךְ עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וְיָמֵשׁ חֹשֶׁךְ (neteh yadkha al-ha-shamayim vihi choshekh al-erets Mitsrayim, "stretch out your hand toward the heavens, and there shall be darkness upon the land of Mitsrayim"; ve-yamesh choshekh, "and one may feel darkness / and darkness may be felt"). **Key phrase:** וְיָמֵשׁ חֹשֶׁךְ (ve-yamesh choshekh) is notoriously difficult. The verb יָמֵשׁ (yamesh) likely derives from מושׁ/משׁשׁ (mush/mashash, "to feel, grope") — meaning the darkness is so thick it can be physically felt/touched. Alternatively, some read it as from מוּשׁ (mush, "to depart, remove") with ironic force: "and darkness may depart [i.e., displace the light]." The traditional reading "darkness that may be felt" captures the supernatural, tangible quality of this plague. This plague strikes at Egypt's supreme deity Ra/Amon-Ra, the sun god. LXX: καὶ γενηθήτω σκότος ἐπὶ γῆν Αἰγύπτου, ψηλαφητὸν σκότος ("and let there be darkness upon the land of Egypt, a darkness to be felt/groped") — ψηλαφητός ("touchable, palpable") from ψηλαφάω ("to grope, feel"), confirming the tactile reading.

**22** And Mosheh stretched out his hand toward the heavens, and there was afelah-choshekh [thick darkness] in all the land of Mitsrayim [for] three days.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־הַשָּׁמָיִם וַיְהִי חֹשֶׁךְ־אֲפֵלָה בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים (vayyet Mosheh et-yado al-ha-shamayim, "and Mosheh stretched out his hand toward the heavens"; vayehi choshekh-afelah be-khol-erets Mitsrayim, "and there was thick darkness in all the land of Mitsrayim"; sheloshet yamim, "three days"). **Key term:** אֲפֵלָה (afelah) = "thick darkness, deep gloom" — from the root אפל ("to be dark, gloomy"). This is the intensified form: not merely choshekh ("darkness") but choshekh-afelah ("darkness of thick gloom"). The combination appears in Joel 2:2 and Zephaniah 1:15 for the Day of YHWH. Three days of absolute darkness — an assault on Egyptian cosmology where the sun's nightly journey through the underworld was the central drama of existence. LXX: καὶ ἐγένετο σκότος γνόφος θύελλα ἐπὶ πᾶσαν γῆν Αἰγύπτου τρεῖς ἡμέρας ("and there was darkness, gloom, storm upon all the land of Egypt three days") — γνόφος ("cloud, darkness") + θύελλα ("storm") expand afelah.

**23** They did not see, each man his brother, and they did not rise, each man from his place, [for] three days; but for all the sons of Yisra'el there was light in their dwellings.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא־רָאוּ אִישׁ אֶת־אָחִיו וְלֹא־קָמוּ אִישׁ מִתַּחְתָּיו שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים וּלְכָל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הָיָה אוֹר בְּמוֹשְׁבֹתָם (lo-ra'u ish et-akhiv, "they did not see, each man his brother"; ve-lo-qamu ish mi-takhtav, "and they did not rise, each man from beneath him/from his place"; sheloshet yamim, "three days"; u-le-khol-benei Yisra'el hayah or be-moshvotam, "but for all the sons of Yisra'el there was light in their dwellings"). **Yisra'el/Mitsrayim distinction:** As with previous plagues (8:22, 9:4, 9:26), Yisra'el is exempted. The contrast is absolute: total immobilizing darkness for Mitsrayim, light (אוֹר, or) for Yisra'el. The word מוֹשְׁבֹתָם (moshvotam) = "their dwellings" from יָשַׁב (yashav, "to sit, dwell"). The phrase מִתַּחְתָּיו (mi-takhtav, "from beneath him") suggests total paralysis — they could not even stand up. LXX: καὶ οὐκ εἶδεν οὐδεὶς τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ("and no one saw his brother"); πᾶσι δὲ τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ἦν φῶς ἐν πᾶσι οἷς κατεγίνοντο ("but for all the sons of Israel there was light in all where they dwelt").

**24** And Par'oh called to Mosheh and said: "Go, serve YHWH; only your flocks and your herds shall be set [left behind]; also your little ones shall go with you."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקְרָא פַרְעֹה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמֶר לְכוּ עִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה רַק צֹאנְכֶם וּבְקַרְכֶם יֻצָּג גַּם־טַפְּכֶם יֵלֵךְ עִמָּכֶם (lekhu ivdu et-YHWH, "go, serve YHWH"; raq tso'nkhem u-veqarkhem yutsag, "only your flocks and your herds shall be set/stationed [behind]"; gam-tappekhem yelekh immakhem, "also your little ones shall go with you"). **Negotiation escalation:** Par'oh concedes children (cf. v.11 where he refused) but now withholds livestock. The verb יֻצָּג (yutsag) = hophal of יצג/נצג, "shall be set, stationed, left" — the livestock must remain as hostage/surety. LXX: πορευθέντες λατρεύσατε κυρίῳ τῷ θεῷ ὑμῶν· πλὴν τῶν προβάτων καὶ τῶν βοῶν ὑπολίπεσθε ("go and serve the Lord your God; but leave behind the sheep and cattle") — ὑπολείπω ("leave behind") for yutsag.

**25** And Mosheh said: "Also you shall give into our hand sacrifices [zevakhim] and burnt offerings [olot], and we shall make [them] to YHWH our God.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה גַּם־אַתָּה תִּתֵּן בְּיָדֵנוּ זְבָחִים וְעֹלוֹת וְעָשִׂינוּ לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ (gam-attah titten be-yadenu zevakhim ve-olot, "also you shall give into our hand sacrifices and burnt offerings"; ve-asinu la-YHWH Eloheinu, "and we shall make [them] to YHWH our God"). **Mosheh's audacity:** Not only will the Israelites take their own livestock, but Par'oh himself must contribute sacrificial animals. The word זְבָחִים (zevakhim) = "slaughter-sacrifices" (from זבח, zavakh, "to slaughter"); עֹלוֹת (olot) = "burnt offerings" (from עלה, alah, "to go up" — offerings that ascend in smoke). LXX: καὶ σὺ δώσεις ἡμῖν ὁλοκαυτώματα καὶ θυσίας ("and you shall give us whole burnt offerings and sacrifices").

**26** And also our livestock shall go with us; there shall not remain a hoof, for from it we shall take to serve YHWH our God, and we — we do not know with what we shall serve YHWH until we come there."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְגַם־מִקְנֵנוּ יֵלֵךְ עִמָּנוּ לֹא תִשָּׁאֵר פַּרְסָה כִּי מִמֶּנּוּ נִקַּח לַעֲבֹד אֶת־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ וַאֲנַחְנוּ לֹא־נֵדַע מַה־נַּעֲבֹד אֶת־יְהוָה עַד־בֹּאֵנוּ שָׁמָּה (ve-gam-miqnenu yelekh immanu, "and also our livestock shall go with us"; lo tisha'er parsah, "there shall not remain a hoof"; ki mimmennu niqqakh la'avod et-YHWH Eloheinu, "for from it we shall take to serve YHWH our God"; va-anakhnu lo-neda' mah-na'avod et-YHWH ad-bo'enu shammah, "and we — we do not know with what we shall serve YHWH until we come there"). **Key phrase:** לֹא תִשָּׁאֵר פַּרְסָה (lo tisha'er parsah, "not a hoof shall remain") — a vivid synecdoche: not even the smallest part of any animal stays behind. The claim of not knowing what YHWH will require is both theologically honest (they await divine instruction) and a shrewd negotiation tactic. LXX: οὐ καταλείψομεν ὁπλήν ("we will not leave behind a hoof") — ὁπλή for parsah.

**27** And YHWH strengthened [chazaq] the heart of Par'oh, and he was not willing to send them out.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא אָבָה לְשַׁלְּחָם (vayekhazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh ve-lo avah le-shalkham, "and YHWH strengthened the heart of Par'oh, and he was not willing to send them out"). **HARDENING VERB: chazaq (חזק) = "strengthen." Piel וַיְחַזֵּק — YHWH is the AGENT.** Third instance of divine active hardening (after 9:12, 10:20). The verb אָבָה (avah) = "to be willing, consent" — with לֹא, "he was not willing." This adds a volitional dimension: despite everything, Par'oh does not will to release them. LXX: καὶ ἐσκλήρυνεν κύριος τὴν καρδίαν Φαραω, καὶ οὐκ ἐβουλήθη ἐξαποστεῖλαι αὐτούς ("and the Lord hardened the heart of Pharao, and he did not wish to send them out") — βούλομαι ("wish, will") for avah.

**28** And Par'oh said to him: "Go from me! Guard yourself — do not again see my face, for in the day you see my face, you shall die."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר־לוֹ פַרְעֹה לֵךְ מֵעָלָי הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ אַל־תֹּסֶף רְאוֹת פָּנַי כִּי בְּיוֹם רְאֹתְךָ פָנַי תָּמוּת (lekh me-alai, "go from upon me/away from me"; hishamer lekha, "guard yourself"; al-tosef re'ot panai, "do not again see my face"; ki be-yom re'otkha fanai tamut, "for in the day of your seeing my face, you shall die"). **Par'oh's final threat:** The dramatic irony is immense — Par'oh threatens death, but it is his own firstborn who will die (ch. 12). The phrase רְאוֹת פָּנַי (re'ot panai, "seeing my face") = having audience with the king. The verb הִשָּׁמֶר (hishamer) = niphal imperative of שׁמר, "guard yourself" — a warning formula. LXX: ἄπελθε ἀπ᾽ ἐμοῦ· πρόσεχε σεαυτῷ ἔτι προσθεῖναι ἰδεῖν μου τὸ πρόσωπον· ᾗ δ᾽ ἂν ἡμέρᾳ ὀφθῇς μοι, ἀποθανῇ ("depart from me! Take heed to yourself — do not again see my face; for on whatever day you appear before me, you shall die").

**29** And Mosheh said: "You have spoken rightly — I will not again see your face."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה כֵּן דִּבַּרְתָּ לֹא־אֹסִף עוֹד רְאוֹת פָּנֶיךָ (ken dibbarta, "rightly/correctly you have spoken"; lo-osif od re'ot paneicha, "I will not again see your face"). **Mosheh's final word to Par'oh:** The affirmation כֵּן דִּבַּרְתָּ (ken dibbarta, "you have spoken correctly") is both agreement and prophetic irony — Mosheh will indeed not return to Par'oh's presence to negotiate again. The next encounter will be the death of the firstborn (11:4–8, though 11:4–8 may be spoken during this same audience before Mosheh departs). After this, Par'oh will summon Mosheh in the night (12:31). LXX: εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς· εἴρηκας· οὐκέτι ὀφθήσομαί σοι εἰς πρόσωπον ("and Moses said: 'You have spoken — I will no longer appear before your face'").

---

## Hardening-Verb Tracking — Exodus 10

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Verb Form | Translation | Agent |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 10:1 | **כבד (kaved)** | **Hiphil הִכְבַּדְתִּי** | **"I have made heavy"** | **YHWH (first divine kaved-hardening!)** |
| 10:20 | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |
| 10:27 | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |

### Cumulative Hardening-Verb Summary (Exodus 7–10)

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Verb Form | Translation | Agent |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 7:3 | קשה (qashah) | Hiphil impf. אַקְשֶׁה | "I will make hard" | YHWH (predictive) |
| 7:13 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| 7:14 | כבד (kaved) | Adjective כָּבֵד | "[is] heavy" | Par'oh's heart (state) |
| 7:22 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| 8:15 | כבד (kaved) | Hiphil inf. abs. הַכְבֵּד | "he made heavy" | Par'oh (self) |
| 8:19 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| 8:32 | כבד (kaved) | Hiphil וַיַּכְבֵּד | "he made heavy" | Par'oh (self) |
| 9:7 | כבד (kaved) | Qal וַיִּכְבַּד | "was heavy" | Par'oh's heart (stative) |
| 9:12 | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH (first divine chazaq)** |
| 9:34 | כבד (kaved) | Hiphil וַיַּכְבֵּד | "he made heavy" | Par'oh (self) |
| 9:35 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| **10:1** | **כבד (kaved)** | **Hiphil הִכְבַּדְתִּי** | **"I have made heavy"** | **YHWH (first divine kaved!)** |
| **10:20** | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |
| **10:27** | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |

**Pattern note — DIVINE AGENCY DOMINATES:** In Exodus 10, every hardening verb has YHWH as the agent. There is no instance of Par'oh hardening himself. The shift that began in 9:12 now becomes total. In 10:1, YHWH claims the kaved root for the first time — "I have made heavy his heart" (הִכְבַּדְתִּי, hikhbadti) — which was previously only used of Par'oh's self-hardening (8:15, 8:32, 9:34). YHWH now owns both hardening verbs: kaved (10:1) and chazaq (10:20, 10:27). The theological trajectory is clear: after Par'oh repeatedly hardened himself (chs. 7–9), YHWH has taken over entirely. Human agency in the hardening has ceased; divine sovereignty over Par'oh's resistance is now complete. Yet Par'oh's continued negotiation (vv. 8–11, 24) and rage (v. 28) show that divine hardening does not eliminate Par'oh's will — it confirms and intensifies the trajectory Par'oh himself initiated.

## Key Lexical Notes — Exodus 10

- **arbeh** (אַרְבֶּה, vv. 4, 12–15, 19): "locust, swarming locust" — from רבה (ravah, "to be many"). The eighth plague. Described as כָּבֵד מְאֹד ("very heavy," v.14) and unprecedented in scope. The locust swarm covers the "eye of the land" and produces darkness (v.15), foreshadowing the ninth plague.
- **choshekh** (חֹשֶׁךְ, vv. 21–22): "darkness" — the ninth plague. Directly assaults Ra/Amon-Ra, the Egyptian sun deity. The phrase וְיָמֵשׁ חֹשֶׁךְ (ve-yamesh choshekh, v.21) = "darkness that may be felt" — a supernatural, palpable darkness.
- **afelah** (אֲפֵלָה, v. 22): "thick darkness, deep gloom" — intensifies choshekh. The compound חֹשֶׁךְ־אֲפֵלָה (choshekh-afelah) = "darkness of thick gloom." Echoes in prophetic Day of YHWH language (Joel 2:2, Zephaniah 1:15).
- **hit'alalti** (הִתְעַלַּלְתִּי, v. 2): "I have wrought / made sport of" — hitpael of עלל. YHWH's actions in Mitsrayim are both judgment and mockery of Egyptian power. The purpose is intergenerational: "tell in the ears of your son and the son of your son."
- **moqesh** (מוֹקֵשׁ, v. 7): "snare, trap" — Par'oh's servants call the Israelite situation a snare. From יקשׁ ("to snare, lay bait").
- **Yam Suf** (יַם־סוּף, v. 19): "Sea of Reeds" — the destination where the locusts are driven. This name anticipates the crossing in ch. 14–15. סוּף (suf) = "reed, rush" (cf. the suf-basket of Mosheh in 2:3).
- **parsah** (פַּרְסָה, v. 26): "hoof" — from פרס ("to divide, split"). "Not a hoof shall remain" — a vivid synecdoche for complete departure with all livestock.
- **kaved saturation:** The root כבד continues to saturate the narrative: YHWH makes heavy Par'oh's heart (v. 1), the locust plague is "very heavy" (v. 14), the rescue wind is "very strong" (כָּבֵד מְאֹד, v. 19, using kaved for the wind's force). The heaviness of judgment mirrors the heaviness of resistance.
- **Goshen/Yisra'el distinction** (v. 23): Continues through the darkness plague — "for all the sons of Yisra'el there was light in their dwellings" while Mitsrayim was paralyzed in impenetrable darkness.
- **re'ot panai** (רְאוֹת פָּנַי, v. 28): "seeing my face" — Par'oh's prohibition against further audience. The irony: Par'oh bans Mosheh from his presence, but YHWH's next act (the death of the firstborn) will bring Par'oh to summon Mosheh in the night (12:31).
